steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: The sentence pairs two countries with two prices: 62 USD in one location and 110 USD in Luxembourg (marked by “respectively”). The blank comes right after “OECD” and must allow the sentence to name which OECD nations are being priced. The phrase “and Luxembourg” follows immediately, so the blank must complete “OECD ______” as a single unit that identifies the first nation or nations.

steps.title: Apply the appositive punctuation rule
steps.content:
The structure “nations, Chile and Luxembourg” uses an appositive—a noun phrase that renames or clarifies the preceding noun. The comma after “nations” introduces the clarifying examples “Chile and Luxembourg” that specify which OECD nations are meant. This is the standard punctuation rule for appositives. A semicolon (B) separates independent clauses, which these are not. A colon (C) can introduce a list but is less standard for this construction. No punctuation (D) leaves “nations” and “Chile” grammatically unattached, creating an error.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
